title: Environmental impacts of different types of grocery bags
subtitle: Number of times a given grocery bag type would have to be reused to have an environmental impact as low as a standard single-use plastic bag.
note: Average across all indicators includes other metrics not shown here, including marine eutrophication, particulate matter, toxicity and others.
caption:
Environmental impacts of different types of grocery bags

Number of times a given grocery bag type would have to be reused to have an environmental impact as low as a standard single-use plastic bag.

This chart presents a series of horizontal bar graphs comparing the reuse requirements for eight different grocery bag types across various environmental impact categories. The data indicates the number of times a specific bag must be used to match the environmental footprint of a standard single-use plastic bag. Organic cotton displays the highest reuse thresholds, particularly for ozone depletion, which requires 20,000 uses, and water use at 3,830 uses. Conventional cotton also shows high values, with ozone depletion requiring 7,069 uses and water use at 1,358 uses. In contrast, Recycled PET and Polypropylene, woven, recycled bags show significantly lower reuse requirements, generally falling below 100 uses across most indicators. For Recycled PET, freshwater eutrophication requires 96 uses, while greenhouse gas emissions require only 8 uses. Paper-based options, including Bleached paper and Unbleached paper, cluster in the lower-to-mid range, with freshwater eutrophication requiring 43 uses for both types. Biopolymer bags show varied results, with freshwater eutrophication at 41.8 uses but zero uses for greenhouse gas emissions and negative 2.3 uses for water use. Each bag type includes a bar for the average across all indicators, which ranges from 8 uses for Recycled PET to 2,375 uses for Organic cotton. The note clarifies that this average metric incorporates additional factors not explicitly graphed, such as marine eutrophication, particulate matter, and toxicity. The visual arrangement allows for a direct comparison of how different materials perform relative to one another within specific environmental categories.
